melanoma.caption: Dermoscopy shows an asymmetric lesion with irregular pigment network, multiple colors including dark brown, black and blue-gray areas, atypical streaks, regression structures and poorly defined borders, consistent with melanoma and requiring urgent clinical evaluation.
melanoma.cause: Melanoma develops when melanocytes acquire DNA mutations—most often from ultraviolet (UV) radiation—leading to uncontrolled growth and potential spread.
melanoma.risk_factors: Fair skin, heavy UV exposure or tanning beds, blistering sunburns, many/atypical moles, family history of melanoma, and certain genetic syndromes.
melanoma.dermoscopic_features: Asymmetry, multiple colors (brown, black, blue-gray), atypical network/streaks, regression structures, and irregular borders are typical.
melanoma.typical_locations: Sun-exposed areas (back, legs, arms, face), but can occur on non–sun-exposed sites including palms/soles and mucosa.
melanoma.symptoms_signs: ABCDE changes (Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolving), new or changing lesion, bleeding or itch.
melanoma.prevention: Broad-spectrum sunscreen, protective clothing, shade-seeking, avoid tanning beds, routine skin self-exams and periodic professional checks.
melanoma.when_to_seek_care: Immediately if a mole changes in size, shape, color, or symptoms (itch, bleed), or fits ABCDE—prompt dermatology assessment is critical.
melanoma.treatment: Early-stage: surgical excision (often curative). Advanced: immunotherapy, targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK), radiation, and selective chemotherapy depending on stage.
melanoma.prognosis: Highly stage-dependent; early detection yields excellent outcomes, while advanced disease requires systemic therapy with variable response.
melanoma.patient_tips: Photograph moles for comparison over time, learn ABCDE, set reminders for seasonal skin checks, and use SPF 30+ daily.

nv.caption: Benign melanocytic nevus showing a symmetric and organized dermoscopic pattern with a uniform pigment network or homogeneous brown coloration without atypical structures.
nv.cause: Clusters of melanocytes form localized growths due to genetics and developmental factors; UV exposure can influence the number over time.
nv.risk_factors: Genetic predisposition, lighter skin tones, childhood sun exposure; having many nevi modestly increases melanoma risk.
nv.dermoscopic_features: Symmetry, organized pattern, uniform network or homogeneous brown color without atypical structures.
nv.typical_locations: Anywhere on skin; common on trunk and limbs, can be congenital or acquired.
nv.symptoms_signs: Usually asymptomatic, stable in color and shape; concerning signs follow ABCDE if they evolve.
nv.prevention: Sun protection in childhood/adulthood and routine monitoring reduces the chance of atypical changes.
nv.when_to_seek_care: If a mole changes (size, shape, color), looks different from others (“ugly duckling”), or bleeds/itches persistently.
nv.treatment: No treatment needed if typical; excision for cosmetic reasons or pathology if atypical/suspicious.
nv.prognosis: Benign; excellent outlook. Atypical changes warrant biopsy to exclude melanoma.
nv.patient_tips: Track new moles, avoid peak sun, teach family the ABCDE checklist, and don’t pick or irritate moles.

bkl.full_name: Benign keratosis-like lesion (seborrheic keratosis, solar lentigo, LPLK)
bkl.caption: Seborrheic keratosis-like lesion showing a sharply demarcated border, milia-like cysts, comedo-like openings and cerebriform surface pattern consistent with benign keratosis.
bkl.cause: Proliferation of keratinocytes with aging and genetics; not precancerous though may mimic other lesions clinically.
bkl.risk_factors: Increasing age, family tendency; appear in mid to later adulthood and become more numerous with age.
bkl.dermoscopic_features: Sharply demarcated borders, milia-like cysts, comedo-like openings, cerebriform/brain-like surface, moth-eaten edges.
bkl.typical_locations: Face, chest, back, shoulders; any body site except palms/soles; often multiple.
bkl.symptoms_signs: Waxy, “stuck-on” papules/plaques, brown to black color, may itch or get irritated by friction.
bkl.prevention: No proven prevention; sun protection helps differentiate from pigmented lesions that require evaluation.
bkl.when_to_seek_care: If rapidly changing, inflamed, bleeding, or uncertain diagnosis—dermatology review to exclude malignancy.
bkl.treatment: No treatment needed unless symptomatic/cosmetic: cryotherapy, curettage, shave excision, or laser.
bkl.prognosis: Benign; removal is curative if desired; lesions may recur elsewhere with aging.
bkl.patient_tips: Avoid picking; use emollients for irritation; get a check if a lesion looks different from your usual ones.

bcc.caption: Basal cell carcinoma displaying arborizing telangiectasia, shiny pearly surface, irregular vessel pattern, ulceration, and blue-gray globules, suggestive of a malignant lesion requiring biopsy.
bcc.cause: Chronic UV exposure causes DNA damage in basal epidermal cells; cumulative sun over years is the major driver.
bcc.risk_factors: Fair skin, outdoor/occupational sun, older age, prior radiation, personal skin cancer history, immunosuppression.
bcc.dermoscopic_features: Arborizing vessels, translucent/pearly surface, ulceration, blue-gray ovoid nests/globules, shiny areas.
bcc.typical_locations: Head and neck (face, nose, ears), shoulders/upper trunk—areas with heavy sun exposure.
bcc.symptoms_signs: Non-healing shiny papule/patch, may bleed or crust, slowly enlarging, sometimes tender.
bcc.prevention: UV avoidance, sunscreen, protective clothing, regular exams especially after prior skin cancers.
bcc.when_to_seek_care: Any persistent, pearly or bleeding spot that doesn’t heal over weeks; prompt biopsy is indicated.
bcc.treatment: Surgical excision or Mohs micrographic surgery first-line; alternatives include curettage/cryotherapy, topical agents, radiation, hedgehog-pathway inhibitors for advanced disease.
bcc.prognosis: Excellent with local therapy; metastasis is rare but neglected lesions can be locally destructive.
bcc.patient_tips: Schedule routine checks after one BCC; document lesions with photos; practice daily sun protection.

akiec.full_name: Actinic keratosis / intraepidermal carcinoma (SCC in situ)
akiec.caption: Actinic keratosis or intraepidermal carcinoma showing erythematous base, scaly or crusted surface, dotted vessels, and atypical follicular openings on sun-damaged skin; considered precancerous or early carcinoma in situ.
akiec.cause: Long-term UV radiation damages keratinocytes, producing dysplasia that can progress to squamous cell carcinoma if untreated.
akiec.risk_factors: Chronic sun exposure, fair skin, older age, immunosuppression, outdoor work, tanning beds.
akiec.dermoscopic_features: Erythematous background, adherent scale, dotted/linear vessels, rosettes and follicular changes on facial AK.
akiec.typical_locations: Face, scalp, ears, lips, forearms, hands—chronically sun-exposed sites.
akiec.symptoms_signs: Rough, sandpaper-like patch or scaly plaque; may be tender or burn; often multiple on photodamaged skin.
akiec.prevention: Consistent broad-spectrum sunscreen, sun-protective clothing, shade, lip SPF; avoid tanning devices.
akiec.when_to_seek_care: New or persistent scaly red patches on sun-exposed skin; evaluation is important due to progression risk.
akiec.treatment: Cryotherapy for individual lesions; field therapy with topical 5-FU or imiquimod, photodynamic therapy; excision for suspected SCC in situ.
akiec.prognosis: Excellent with treatment; untreated AK carries a measurable risk of evolving to invasive SCC over time.
akiec.patient_tips: Treat whole sun-damaged areas (“field therapy”) when multiple AKs exist; maintain strict photoprotection year-round.

df.caption: Dermatofibroma presenting with a central white scar-like structure, peripheral delicate pigment network, and firm nodular appearance typical of a benign fibrohistiocytic lesion.
df.cause: Likely reactive fibrous tissue proliferation after minor trauma (e.g., insect bite), though exact cause is uncertain.
df.risk_factors: More frequent in adults and women; can occur in immunocompromised individuals.
df.dermoscopic_features: Central white scar-like area with peripheral delicate network; ‘dimple sign’ on lateral compression is classic.
df.typical_locations: Lower legs, arms, upper back; solitary firm papule/nodule 0.5–1.5 cm.
df.symptoms_signs: Firm, mobile papule; may be pruritic or tender; often stable for years.
df.prevention: No established prevention; general skin care and avoiding repetitive trauma may help.
df.when_to_seek_care: If rapidly growing, painful, atypical appearance, or diagnostic uncertainty—biopsy to exclude mimics.
df.treatment: Observation if asymptomatic; shave or excision for pain/irritation or cosmetic reasons.
df.prognosis: Benign with excellent outlook; recurrence uncommon after complete removal.
df.patient_tips: Avoid rubbing or shaving over the bump; monitor for changes; seek evaluation if atypical features develop.

vasc.caption: Vascular lesion characterized by well-defined red, purple or blue homogeneous areas with lacunae and absence of pigment network, consistent with angioma or vascular malformation.
vasc.cause: Benign proliferation or clustering of blood vessels; can be congenital or appear with aging and hormonal changes.
vasc.risk_factors: Age-related appearance (cherry angiomas), pregnancy/hormonal shifts; some malformations are congenital.
vasc.dermoscopic_features: Red-blue homogeneous areas, multiple lacunae, no pigment network; spider angioma shows central arteriole with radiating vessels.
vasc.typical_locations: Trunk and limbs (cherry angiomas), face (spider angioma); can occur widely on skin.
vasc.symptoms_signs: Usually asymptomatic; may bleed if traumatized; spider angiomas can reflect systemic or hepatic factors in some cases.
vasc.prevention: No specific prevention; general skin protection and avoiding trauma minimize bleeding risk.
vasc.when_to_seek_care: Rapid changes, frequent bleeding, pain, or numerous spider angiomas—evaluate for underlying conditions if clinically indicated.
vasc.treatment: Often none needed; cosmetic removal with laser, electrocautery, or excision for symptomatic lesions.
vasc.prognosis: Benign with excellent outlook; cosmetic treatments are effective when desired.
vasc.patient_tips: Protect from friction; document changes; discuss cosmetic options if lesions bother you.
